posts.hook: Your eye has a muscle that gets weaker every year you spend staring at screens. This 2-minute exercise rebuilds it.
posts.caption:
Accommodation: your eye's ability to shift focus between near and far.

It's controlled by the ciliary muscle and crystalline lens.

Spend most of your day at arm's length, and this muscle learns to stay contracted.

Symptoms of weakened accommodation:
→ Takes longer to refocus after looking up from your phone
→ Far objects blur briefly when you switch from near
→ Reading fatigue earlier in the day

Focus shifting exercise rebuilds accommodation range.

3 minutes. No equipment.

Guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

#FocusShifting #Accommodation #EyeExercise #Axeo #HealthTok #EyeHealth #VisionTraining

posts.hook: Focus shifting: the exercise that trains your eye's full accommodation range — and why screens are narrowing it.
posts.caption:
Accommodation is your eye's ability to change focus between near and far distances.

It's one of the most remarkable optical systems in biology:
- At 25cm: ciliary muscle fully contracted, lens maximally curved
- At 6m+: muscle fully relaxed, lens flattened
- Transition time: as fast as 300ms in a healthy eye

The problem with screen-dominated days:
You spend 90% of your visual time in a narrow near range (30–60cm).

Your accommodation system adapts — it gets efficient at near work and sluggish at the transitions that matter.

Focus shifting exercise:
1. Hold thumb at arm's length (60cm)
2. Focus on thumb for 5 seconds
3. Look at something across the room (3–5m) for 5 seconds
4. Look out a window (6m+) for 5 seconds
5. Return to thumb — repeat 10 times

This exercises the full accommodation range — near, mid, and far.

Guided version at axeo.vision/exercise

#Accommodation #FocusShifting #EyeExercise #Axeo #AxeoVision #NearFarFocus #EyeHealth

posts.hook: 3 focus distances. 10 reps. 3 minutes. This is the full accommodation exercise.
posts.caption:
Focus shifting trains your eye's near-to-far accommodation range.

Screens keep you locked in one zone (30–60cm) all day.

This exercise uses all three:

1. Near: thumb at 60cm — 5 seconds
2. Mid: object at 3–5m — 5 seconds
3. Far: window or 6m+ — 5 seconds

10 full rounds = 2.5 minutes.

The ciliary muscle gets the full contraction-to-relaxation cycle it never gets from screen work alone.

Guided timer version free at axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: Why your vision blurs briefly when you look up from your phone — and the exercise that fixes it in 3 minutes.
posts.caption:
That half-second blur when you look up from your phone?

Not a disease. Not myopia progressing.

It's a lagging accommodation response.

Your ciliary muscle has been contracted for hours.

When you suddenly ask it to relax (for distance vision), it's slow.

This is accommodation lag — and it's measurable.

If you spend 6+ hours a day on screens, your accommodation system is operating in a compressed range:
→ Rarely asked to relax fully
→ Rarely stressed to its near limit
→ The full arc never exercised

Focus shifting forces the full range:
- Near (thumb, 60cm) → Mid (3–5m) → Far (window, 6m+)
- 5 seconds each zone
- 10 rounds = 2.5 minutes

Most people notice reduced blur lag within 2 weeks of daily practice.

Free guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

#AccommodationLag #FocusShifting #EyeExercise #Axeo #EyeHealth #NearFarFocus #DigitalEyeStrain

posts.hook: The blurry second when you look up from your phone has a name — accommodation lag. Here's the science and the 3-minute fix:
posts.caption:
1/ Accommodation is your eye's ability to change focus between near and far objects. In a healthy eye, this happens in about 300ms. After a day of screen work, it can take 1–3 seconds. That lag is measurable — and improvable.

2/ The mechanism: your ciliary muscle contracts to increase lens curvature for near focus and relaxes to flatten it for distance. Sustained near work keeps this muscle partially contracted for hours. When you suddenly demand full relaxation (to focus far), it's slow to respond.

3/ This is called accommodation infacility — not the same as myopia, though it contributes to temporary pseudomyopia (blur at distance that resolves after a break). It's a muscle performance issue, not a structural one.

4/ The fix: near-far focus shifting. Three zones — near (60cm), mid (3–5m), far (6m+) — held for 5 seconds each. 10 rounds. Total: 2.5 minutes. Forces the full accommodation arc that screen work never demands.

5/ Research on accommodative facility training shows measurable improvements in response speed within 2–4 weeks of daily practice. This is used in sports vision programs for athletes who need fast focal transitions.

6/ Guided version at axeo.vision/exercise — includes the three-zone timer and technique reminders. Free, browser-based.

If that post-phone blur is bothering you, try 10 rounds tonight.

posts.hook: Accommodation infacility — the screen-work vision problem that isn't myopia, but feels like it.
posts.caption:
If your team spends 6–8 hours a day on screens, some of them are experiencing this:

They look up from their laptop. The whiteboard, the colleague across the desk, the clock on the wall — all briefly blurry. Refocuses in 1–3 seconds.

This is not myopia progressing. It's accommodation lag.

The ciliary muscle — which drives focus transitions — is held near-constantly contracted during screen work. When suddenly asked to relax for distance vision, response is slow.

For workers who:
→ Switch frequently between screen and physical materials
→ Attend in-person meetings after long screen sessions
→ Drive after work hours (critical safety concern)

...accommodation lag is a direct performance and safety consideration.

The intervention is straightforward: near-far focus shifting, 3 minutes, daily.

1. Near: 60cm object, 5 seconds
2. Mid: 3–5m object, 5 seconds
3. Far: window or 6m+, 5 seconds
4. 10 rounds

This exercises the full accommodation range that screen-only work never demands.

Axeo provides guided focus training free at axeo.vision/exercise — no download, no signup, runs in any browser.
